text: The surgeon repairs the hiatal hernia by repositioning the stomach below the diaphragm and reducing the size of the diaphragmatic opening.
The surgeon places a mesh over the incision site to reinforce the closure, aiming to minimize the risk of hiatal hernia recurrence.
The surgeon performs the Toupee procedure, wrapping the top part of the stomach partially around the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) to enhance its function.
The surgical footage shows the repair of a hiatal hernia, with the stomach being repositioned below the diaphragm and the diaphragmatic opening being reduced in size.
The surgeon dissects the stomach, esophagus, and hernia sac from the surrounding chest and abdominal soft tissue structures, carefully separating these anatomical components.
During the mobilization of the hernia sac and esophagus, the surgeon carefully identifies the vagal nerves, ensuring they are not injured during the dissection process.
The surgeon mobilizes the esophagus, ensuring that at least three centimeters of the distal esophagus is positioned within the abdominal cavity to prevent recurrence of the hiatal hernia.
The surgeon performs a high mediastinal dissection to achieve the necessary length of the distal esophagus within the abdomen, ensuring proper positioning for the hiatal hernia repair.
The surgeon closes the enlarged diaphragmatic opening, or hiatus, using sutures to achieve a tension-free repair, ensuring the anatomical structures are securely repositioned.
The surgical footage shows the performance of a toupee fundoplication, where the stomach is wrapped around the esophagus to reinforce the lower esophageal sphincter, preventing reflux.
The esophagus and stomach are visibly freed from surrounding tissues, allowing for further surgical manipulation during the hernia repair procedure.
Sutures are visible closing the diaphragmatic opening through which the esophagus passes into the abdominal cavity, ensuring a secure repair.
The surgeon maneuvers the top part of the stomach behind and around the esophagus, visually wrapping it to form a toupee fundoplication.
The surgeon moves the stomach back and forth to ensure it is loose, preventing any restriction of blood flow that could lead to tissue damage.
The surgeon anchors the posterior aspect of the stomach to the diaphragm by placing sutures on either side of the esophagus, securing the stomach in position.
The surgeon places a pair of horizontal mattress sutures anteriorly to complete the toupee fundoplication, securing the stomach around the esophagus.
